topic: Someone receives a package intended for the previous tenant
raw_output:
[story 1]

The knock on the door was a jarring intrusion into my quiet afternoon. I peered through the peephole, my jaw tightening before I even moved. It was a delivery, a brown box sitting awkwardly by the mat. A flicker of annoyance crossed my face; another piece of unwanted correspondence from the past. I pulled the door open just enough to peer out, my expression hardening into something cool and distant. The package was addressed to the former occupant, a faded script that seemed to mock the current reality of this apartment. I took a deep, slow breath, the air feeling suddenly stale. With a sigh that carried the weight of accumulated irritation, I retrieved the box. My fingers brushed the cardboard, a brief, almost imperceptible tightening in my hand before I set it down on the hall table, already planning the most efficient way to dispose of it.

[story 2]

When the buzzer rang, I hesitated, a muscle ticking in my jaw. It was a delivery notification. I didn't need to open it; I could see the sender’s name on the screen. It was clearly meant for the previous renter, someone whose tenancy had long since concluded. A wave of profound boredom washed over me. I dragged myself to the door, my movements sluggish. When I finally pulled the door inward, my eyes swept over the package—a bulky, unremarkable parcel—and I let out a short, sharp exhale. "This isn't for me," I mumbled, my voice flat, devoid of any warmth. I didn't offer to take it, nor did I invite further interaction. I simply gestured toward the hallway, a clear, unspoken command that this object belonged elsewhere, somewhere forgotten.

[story 3]

I watched the delivery person fumble with the intercom, the whole exchange feeling tedious and utterly pointless. When the package was handed over, I felt a distinct clenching in my chest. It was an imposition, an unnecessary reminder of a life I was actively trying to move beyond. I took the box with two fingers, treating it as if it were contaminated. My gaze flickered over the label, registering the former tenant's name with a swift, dismissive sweep. I didn't make eye contact with the courier. "Keep that," I managed, the words clipped and brittle, my tone conveying a profound lack of interest in the contents or the sender. I turned immediately, eager to retreat back into the sanctuary of my own space, the package already feeling like an unwelcome stain on the otherwise clean slate of my living area.
